author: @bearmtn7
text:
There was a time in the Soviet Union where Joseph Stalin was given a standing ovation after a speech for his Communist Party. However, the applause went on much longer than you'd expect. After 11 minutes, one person sat down and stopped applauding; this person was later arrested and imprisoned for 10 years.

I believe the Oscar nominations are in a similar vein; nobody wants to stick their head up and say, "nah, this wasn't very good." Going against the grain would be career suicide. I would venture to say, many of these people actually disliked the film. However, nobody wants to be the first to stop the applause

Good video 👍

Communication is important and we need to respect the rules of language in order to avoid noise and have good communication.

If it was a native speaker who didn't care: I'd say not caring is the beginning of a bigger problem and someone should point it out so the person can at least think about it.

How is this problematic for you?

And also: I wasn't, but I'd been an awesome hall monitor. Autistic people are great for stuff like that when given the chance.

replies.text:
@  @TreyWK  I hope you're not one of those 'TL,DR' people... Anyway, if you're not interested in the conversation, no worries. Here I go: 

I'll try not to get lost in the semantics too much because that's beside the point, but let me try to explain where I'm coming from and you can tell me if you agree or not: 

I say that language is a code because when I say 'house', that word (which is not a symbol. The letters themselves are, but not the word. We don't use letters to communicate, we use words, and we represent them in written form with letters, sure, but that's only a visual aid) brings a specific image to your mind. You picture a certain kind of building with defined characteristics that differ greatly from those that would come to mind if I say the word 'dog' or 'book'. 

I can encode an idea into a series of words which obey a certain structure based on the rules of a determined language, which is English in this case. We can say that 'English' is the code we use to interpret the message. 

You then, after hearing the word or the phrase, use your knowledge of English to decode the message, interpret it, and then come up with an idea for your reply, which you in turn also encode using the same set of rules to send it back to me so we can continue the conversation, being certain that I will understand because we both agree on the rules of the code.

Now, why do I believe that respecting the code is so important? 
I think it is very important because of its impact in our lives. The actions we take are mandated by our thinking, which is shaped by the code in which we mainly think. 
If our minds are messy, it would be no surprise that our actions would be messy too. 

I believe that a structured mind can come up with well thought decisions, which in turn would have a high probability of resulting in desirable outcomes which benefit at least the performer of the action, but potentially the people around them too.

If we allow ourselves to deform language to such a degree that anything means whatever we want, we perpetuate laziness, we become sloppy, the messages become harder to interpret, communication suffers and all of that will end up impacting the quality of human relationships.

Maybe I'm overthinking too much, I do that a lot, but I'm convinced that the correct use of language is important; and I also believe that if something is worth doing, it's worth doing right. 

I'm sorry if I seemed like an a-hole, or if it seemed 'petty' of me to make a correction, but I disagree with at least the last opinion (the first one is subjective. I know my sense of humor might be weird, but my intention was never to insult or demean). I don't think that the incorrect use of language is something that should be considered 'petty' at all... languages shape the world, and just by looking at how lax we have been with the use of language, especially since the invention of the internet, and the current state of the world... I don't know... it's difficult for me not to see a correlation.

Anyway... I don't know if that clarifies things or not, but I'd love to know what you think if you want to share. 

Oh, and about needing to quit being so critical... maybe you're right, but where is the line? Is making a simple correction on a comment section of a YouTube video that bad? Shouldn't we be more critical about everything instead of just censoring, judging and cancelling people? Shouldn't we open the door to dialogue instead of just attacking each other all the time? 

I mean... you yourself criticized amrabdalnasser4691 for having a different opinion than yours about the quality of Joker 2. Why not ask 'why do you think it's not bad?' instead? That way maybe you could at least have a different opinion to complement yours, be better informed, expand your perspective... I don't know.

author: @whitworth5s248
text:
One of my most cringe memories was back in grade school when I was one of the two people who got to pick teams in PE and I picked a girl I liked first instead of going with one of the usual athletic kids, even though she was one of the worst players. I thought she'd see it as sweet or something, but it was so obvious that it was kinda transparent why I did it and so instead of just being a man and telling her I liked her later in private, I made it awkward for everyone involved, my team sucked ass, and I was forever embarrassed.

This is Hollywood picking the worst person possible to play the game in PE because they really REALLY want you to know that they like them.
